title: Defense Capabilities and Military Modernization
data_note: Statistics compiled from publicly available sources. Web verification was unavailable at compilation time; URLs point to known authoritative publication pages. Users should verify current figures at listed sources.

source.name: Taiwan Ministry of National Defense / Executive Yuan Budget

statistics.context: Taiwan's FY2025 defense budget was approximately NT$647 billion (including special budgets), representing about 2.45% of GDP. The base MND budget was approximately NT$471 billion, with additional special procurement budgets. This represents a significant increase from FY2024's NT$606 billion.
statistics.verification_note: Figures from Executive Yuan budget submission and MND public statements; includes special budgets for asymmetric capabilities

statistics.metric: Taiwan defense spending as share of GDP

source.name: Taiwan Ministry of National Defense / DGBAS GDP estimates

statistics.context: When including special procurement budgets, Taiwan's defense spending reaches approximately 2.4-2.5% of GDP. The base MND budget alone is approximately 1.7-1.8% of GDP. Taiwan has been increasing defense spending annually in response to growing PLA threats.
statistics.verification_note: Calculation depends on inclusion/exclusion of special budgets; base budget alone is lower

statistics.metric: Indigenous Defense Submarine (IDS) program - Hai Kun launched

source.name: Taiwan Ministry of National Defense / CSBC Corporation

statistics.context: The first IDS submarine, Hai Kun (SS-711), was launched on September 28, 2023, at CSBC's Kaohsiung shipyard. The 2,500-ton diesel-electric submarine is undergoing sea trials. Taiwan plans to build a total of 8 indigenous submarines to replace its aging fleet of 2 Hai Lung-class (Dutch-built, 1980s) and 2 WWII-era Guppy-class boats. Total program cost is estimated at approximately NT$493.6 billion ($16 billion USD).
statistics.verification_note: Hai Kun launch widely reported by international media; program details from MND and Legislative Yuan budget documents

source.name: CSBC Corporation Taiwan / Jane's Fighting Ships

statistics.context: The Hai Kun has an estimated submerged displacement of approximately 2,500-3,000 tons. Specific capabilities are classified but it is reported to feature modern sonar, torpedoes, and potentially submarine-launched anti-ship missiles (Harpoon variants).
statistics.verification_note: Displacement figures from CSBC unveiling and defense media reporting; exact specifications classified

statistics.context: Taiwan's active-duty military has approximately 170,000-188,000 personnel across Army (~94,000), Navy (~40,000), Air Force (~35,000), and other services. The force has been transitioning from conscription to an all-volunteer force since 2018, though mandatory military service was extended back to one year starting January 2024.
statistics.verification_note: IISS Military Balance is the standard reference; figures also reported by SIPRI and Taiwan MND

source.name: Taiwan Ministry of National Defense / Reserve Command

statistics.context: Taiwan maintains a registered reserve force of approximately 2.3 million. However, actual combat-ready reserves are significantly fewer, estimated at 300,000-500,000 with adequate training. Taiwan has been reforming its reserve system since 2022, extending reserve training from 5-7 days to 14 days annually.
statistics.verification_note: Total registered reserve widely cited; combat-ready subset is an estimate from defense analysts

source.name: Taiwan Ministry of National Defense / Executive Yuan announcement December 2022

statistics.context: President Tsai Ing-wen announced in December 2022 that mandatory military service would be extended from 4 months to 1 year, effective January 1, 2024, for men born after 2005. The extended service includes enhanced combat training modeled partly on U.S. Marine Corps and Ukrainian asymmetric warfare lessons.
statistics.verification_note: Announced by President Tsai on December 27, 2022; implemented January 1, 2024

statistics.metric: Taiwan drone procurement and production plan (announced)

source.name: Taiwan National Chung-Shan Institute of Science and Technology (NCSIST) / MND

statistics.context: Taiwan has accelerated drone procurement following lessons from the Ukraine-Russia war. Plans include procurement of approximately 3,400+ military UAVs across attack, surveillance, and loitering munition categories from 2024-2028. NCSIST is developing indigenous models including the Chien Hsiang (Brave Eagle) loitering munition and Teng Yun MALE UAV. A civilian drone national team of companies has also been established.
statistics.verification_note: Drone numbers from MND budget documents and media briefings; exact procurement numbers evolve with annual budget allocations

statistics.metric: Harpoon coastal defense anti-ship missile systems (on order from U.S.)
statistics.context: In October 2020, DSCA notified Congress of a $2.37 billion sale of 400 Harpoon Coastal Defense Systems (HCDS) and associated equipment to Taiwan. This includes 400 RGM-84L-4 Harpoon Block II surface-launched missiles, 100 Harpoon Block II missiles for air launch, 4 mobile coastal defense batteries, plus radar, C2, and logistics support. Delivery timeline extends through mid-2020s.
statistics.verification_note: DSCA notification to Congress is public record; delivery timeline subject to production schedules

statistics.metric: Taiwan annual arms procurement from the United States (cumulative recent major sales)

source.name: DSCA Major Arms Sales Notifications / Congressional Research Service

statistics.context: U.S. arms sales to Taiwan have accelerated significantly. Major packages include F-16V upgrades ($8B, 2019), Harpoon HCDS ($2.37B, 2020), M1A2T Abrams tanks ($2B, 2019), and various missile defense, drone, and asymmetric systems. The U.S. maintains a substantial backlog of undelivered orders to Taiwan.
statistics.verification_note: Cumulative figure from DSCA notifications; actual deliveries lag behind approved sales

statistics.metric: U.S. arms delivery backlog to Taiwan
statistics.context: As of 2024, the backlog of approved but undelivered U.S. arms to Taiwan is estimated at approximately $19-20 billion. This backlog has drawn significant political attention and concerns about Taiwan's readiness. Key delayed items include F-16V deliveries and various missile systems.
statistics.verification_note: Backlog estimates from CRS reports and CSIS analysis; exact figure varies by source and counting methodology

statistics.metric: Hsiung Feng III supersonic anti-ship missile (indigenous, deployed)
statistics.context: The HF-III is a Mach 2+ ramjet-powered supersonic anti-ship cruise missile developed by NCSIST. It is deployed on multiple naval platforms and coastal launchers. Range is estimated at 300+ km. Taiwan also deploys the subsonic Hsiung Feng II (150 km range) and is developing the Hsiung Feng IIE land-attack variant (1,000+ km range, reported).
statistics.verification_note: Exact specifications classified; range estimates from defense publications and NCSIST exhibitions
